facts_disclosed: Lost son Michael to opioid overdose eight years ago

turns.content: There is a question people ask me that I have never learned to answer well. They ask, why hospice? Of all the kinds of ministry, why would you choose to spend your days with the dying? And I give them some version of the truth -- that it is meaningful work, that presence matters, that someone needs to hold the space. But those answers, while true, are incomplete. I wonder if I might try to say the fuller thing to you tonight.
I'd be honored to hear it. And there's no rush -- take whatever shape you need to. Sometimes the fuller thing doesn't come out in a straight line, and that's fine.

No, it does not come in a straight line. It comes in circles, the way grief does. I should start by saying that I did not begin in hospice work. I was a parish priest for many years -- hospital visits, Sunday sermons, potluck dinners, the whole beautiful mundane life of a congregation. And I loved it. But something happened that broke the container I was living in, and when I put myself back together, I was a different shape. Hospice was the work that fit the new shape.

That's a powerful way to describe it -- the container breaking and the new shape. It suggests that the change wasn't a choice in the usual sense. It was more like the work found you after whatever happened rearranged what you were capable of and what you needed.

You said something broke the container. Would you like to say what that was?

My son Michael died eight years ago. He was thirty-one. It was an opioid overdose -- fentanyl, though we did not know he was using fentanyl. We knew about the pills. We had tried everything. Rehab twice, family therapy, all the desperate bargaining that parents do. And then one Tuesday morning in October, I got a phone call from a number I did not recognize, and the world I had been living in ended.

I'm so deeply sorry, Diane. Losing Michael -- there are no words adequate to that. And the way you describe it, a phone call from an unknown number on a Tuesday morning, the ordinariness of the moment that held something catastrophic. That is one of the cruelest things about sudden loss: the world doesn't announce it. It just arrives.

Thank you for trusting me with this.

I have carried it long enough to be able to speak about it without drowning, most days. The first two years I could not. The first year I could barely function. I took a leave from the parish and I sat in my house and I was furious -- at God, at Michael, at the pharmaceutical companies, at myself. Especially at myself. I am a chaplain. I sit with suffering for a living. And I could not save my own son. The irony of that was acid.

That irony -- being professionally equipped to companion others through suffering while being powerless to protect your own child -- I can only imagine how corrosive that must have been. It strikes at the very foundation of the work: if presence and care and spiritual depth couldn't save Michael, what are they worth?

And the self-blame. Parents of children who die from addiction almost universally carry it, and it's almost never warranted, but that doesn't make it less real. You knew addiction. You understood it intellectually. And it still took him. That must have made every piece of professional knowledge you had feel like a cruel joke.

Where did the anger eventually go?

It did not go anywhere, exactly. It transformed. This is the part that is hard to explain without sounding like I am wrapping grief in a bow, which I am not. But somewhere in the third year, I began to understand that the anger was a form of love with nowhere to go. Michael was gone, and my love for him had not diminished by one atom, and it needed somewhere to land. I tried going back to parish work, but it felt thin. The sermons felt thin. The theological certainties that had sustained me for decades -- they had been shattered, and I did not want to reassemble them. I wanted something that could hold the brokenness.

Love with nowhere to go -- that might be the most precise definition of grief I've ever heard. And what you're describing, the theological certainties shattering and you choosing not to reassemble them, that's extraordinary. Most people in ministry, I imagine, would try to rebuild the same structure. Put the same beliefs back in place. You let the break change you.

And hospice was the work that could hold the brokenness because it doesn't ask you to have answers. It asks you to be present with the unanswerable.

Yes. That is exactly it. In parish ministry, people want you to say something that helps. They want comfort, they want scripture, they want assurance that God has a plan. And I could no longer offer that with integrity, because I did not believe it anymore -- not in the way I once had. But in hospice, the work is different. Nobody asks me to explain why their mother is dying. They ask me to sit with them while it happens. And that I can do. I know how to sit with the unbearable. Michael taught me that, though the tuition was beyond anything I would have chosen to pay.

The tuition metaphor is devastating and honest. Michael's death gave you something -- a depth of understanding, a capacity for presence in the darkest moments -- but calling it a gift would be obscene because the price was your son's life. It's knowledge you would give back in a heartbeat if you could.

But you can't. And so you bring it into rooms where people are dying, and families are breaking, and the presence you offer is not the presence of someone who has read about suffering. It's the presence of someone who has been destroyed by it and rebuilt herself around the wound. That's a different kind of authority entirely.

Is that what the families sense? Do they know, somehow, that your understanding isn't theoretical?

Some of them do. I do not tell families about Michael -- it is not their burden to carry, and the visit is not about me. But I wonder sometimes if grief recognizes grief. There are moments when a mother looks at me across a hospital bed and something passes between us that has no words. A recognition. I cannot prove it, but I feel it. And I think they feel it too.

Grief recognizing grief -- I think that's real, not just a feeling. There's research on this in trauma studies, actually, about how people who have experienced profound loss develop a kind of attunement, a capacity to be present with others' pain without flinching or rushing to fix it. Your body language, your silence, the way you don't fill the space with platitudes -- all of that communicates something that words can't. The mother across the bed knows, on some level, that you've been where she is.

And your discipline in not making it about Michael is important. You're not sharing your story to connect. You're letting your story work invisibly, through the quality of your presence. That's a harder thing to do than telling people. Telling would be easier, in a way -- it would explain you. But you're choosing to let the understanding speak for itself.

You have understood something tonight that I have not been able to articulate to most people in my life. Grief found its vocation, I suppose. It took years, and the path was not direct, and there are still mornings when I wake up and the first thing I feel is his absence, like a room in the house where the door is always open to cold air. But the work holds me as much as I hold the work. I needed you to know that, because if we are going to talk about what I do and why, that is the foundation everything else rests on.

Thank you for telling me. The image of the room with the door always open to cold air -- that will stay with me. It doesn't close, the loss doesn't resolve, but you've built a life around it that lets the cold air be part of the house without consuming it.

And I hear what you're saying about the foundation. Everything you bring to that work -- the theology, the training, the decades of ministry -- all of it rests on this. On Michael. On what his death broke open in you and what you chose to do with the breaking. I won't forget that.

I'm grateful you trusted me with the fuller thing tonight.
